Despite these simplifications, the extent to which \(S_j\) can be efficiently characterized remains an open question, which we leave for future work.
title: Computational complexity of optimizing large-market obviously strategy-proof trading mechanisms
background_latex:
Consider a task-allocation problem with a finite set of task types \(i\in\{1,\dots,I\}\) and a finite set of agents \(j\in\{1,\dots,J\}\). Let \(n_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\) denote the total number of instances of task \(i\). An (expected) assignment for agent \(j\) is a vector \(x_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\), where \(x_j(i)\) is the expected number of tasks of type \(i\) assigned to \(j\). The designer has a known cost (or negative value) vector \(\pi_j\in\mathbb{R}^I\), so the designer’s total expected social cost is \(\sum_{j\in J}\pi_j\cdot x_j\). Each agent \(j\) has a random private cost vector \(c_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) (drawn independently across \(j\) from a distribution with full support on a hypercube), and the agent prefers lower workload \(c_j\cdot x_j\). The status quo (outside option) is an endowment assignment \(\eta_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) for each agent.

A (single-agent) polarized-ray menu for agent \(j\) consists of a finite set of trading sets \(\{T^k_j\}_{k\in K_j}\) such that either:

1) a remedial menu \(K_j=\{0,1\}\) with \(T^0_j=\{\eta_j\}\) and \(T^1_j=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}: x\le \eta_j\}\), or

2) each \(T^k_j\) is a non-monotone ray with endpoint \(\eta_j\), i.e.
\[T^k_j=\{\eta_j+y\kappa^k_j: y\in\mathbb{R}_{+}\}\]
for some direction \(\kappa^k_j\in\mathbb{R}^I\) with \(\kappa^k_j\not\ge 0\) and \(\kappa^k_j\not\le 0\), and the collection of directions is polarized in the sense that for any \(k',k''\in K_j\) there exist scalars \(y',y''\ge 0\), not both zero, such that \(y'\kappa^{k'}_j+y''\kappa^{k''}_j\ge 0\) (componentwise).

Given such a menu, the agent’s (obviously strategy-proof) behavior is myopic: for each realized \(c_j\), the agent chooses an index \(k\in K_j\) that minimizes \(\min\{c_j\cdot x: x\in T^k_j\}\). The designer then selects an assignment point \(P_j(k)\in T^k_j\). In the large-market relaxation, feasibility requires only market clearing in expectation:
\[\sum_{j\in J}\mathbb{E}[P_j(s_j(c_j))] = (n_1,\dots,n_I).\]
Define \(F_j\subset\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) as the convex hull of all expected assignments \(\mathbb{E}[P_j(s_j(c_j))]\) achievable for agent \(j\) by varying \(\{T^k_j\}_{k\in K_j}\), the myopically-optimal strategy \(s_j\), and the selection rule \(P_j\), subject to the polarized-ray (or remedial) restriction above. Let \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y: y\in F_j\}\) be the support function of \(F_j\).

The large-market OSP design problem can be written as the convex program
\[\min_{(x_j)_{j\in J}}\ \sum_{j\in J}\pi_j\cdot x_j\quad\text{s.t.}\quad x_j\in F_j\ \forall j,\ \sum_{j\in J}x_j=(n_1,\dots,n_I),\]
with dual
\[\sup_{\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^I}\ \sum_{i=1}^I\lambda_i n_i-\sum_{j\in J}S_j(\lambda-\pi_j).\]
Evaluating or optimizing over the mechanism for each agent \(j\) therefore reduces to characterizing the support function \(S_j\), i.e. solving an optimization problem whose decision variables are menus of polarized rays and selection rules.
problem_definition_latex: Determine whether the optimization problem of characterizing \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y: y\in F_j\}\) (equivalently, optimizing over polarized-ray menus and selection rules for a given agent \(j\) in the large-market relaxation) can be solved efficiently as a function of \((w,\eta_j,\text{the distribution of }c_j)\). In particular, establish the computational complexity of computing \(S_j(w)\) (e.g. polynomial-time solvable vs. NP-hard) and/or design an algorithm with provable complexity guarantees for approximating \(S_j(w)\) to a specified accuracy.
latex:
Consider a task-allocation problem with a finite set of task types \(i\in\{1,\dots,I\}\) and a finite set of agents \(j\in\{1,\dots,J\}\). Let \(n_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\) denote the total number of instances of task \(i\). An (expected) assignment for agent \(j\) is a vector \(x_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\), where \(x_j(i)\) is the expected number of tasks of type \(i\) assigned to \(j\). The designer has a known cost (or negative value) vector \(\pi_j\in\mathbb{R}^I\), so the designer’s total expected social cost is \(\sum_{j\in J}\pi_j\cdot x_j\). Each agent \(j\) has a random private cost vector \(c_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) (drawn independently across \(j\) from a distribution with full support on a hypercube), and the agent prefers lower workload \(c_j\cdot x_j\). The status quo (outside option) is an endowment assignment \(\eta_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) for each agent.

A (single-agent) polarized-ray menu for agent \(j\) consists of a finite set of trading sets \(\{T^k_j\}_{k\in K_j}\) such that either:

1) a remedial menu \(K_j=\{0,1\}\) with \(T^0_j=\{\eta_j\}\) and \(T^1_j=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}: x\le \eta_j\}\), or

2) each \(T^k_j\) is a non-monotone ray with endpoint \(\eta_j\), i.e.
\[T^k_j=\{\eta_j+y\kappa^k_j: y\in\mathbb{R}_{+}\}\]
for some direction \(\kappa^k_j\in\mathbb{R}^I\) with \(\kappa^k_j\not\ge 0\) and \(\kappa^k_j\not\le 0\), and the collection of directions is polarized in the sense that for any \(k',k''\in K_j\) there exist scalars \(y',y''\ge 0\), not both zero, such that \(y'\kappa^{k'}_j+y''\kappa^{k''}_j\ge 0\) (componentwise).

Given such a menu, the agent’s (obviously strategy-proof) behavior is myopic: for each realized \(c_j\), the agent chooses an index \(k\in K_j\) that minimizes \(\min\{c_j\cdot x: x\in T^k_j\}\). The designer then selects an assignment point \(P_j(k)\in T^k_j\). In the large-market relaxation, feasibility requires only market clearing in expectation:
\[\sum_{j\in J}\mathbb{E}[P_j(s_j(c_j))] = (n_1,\dots,n_I).\]
Define \(F_j\subset\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) as the convex hull of all expected assignments \(\mathbb{E}[P_j(s_j(c_j))]\) achievable for agent \(j\) by varying \(\{T^k_j\}_{k\in K_j}\), the myopically-optimal strategy \(s_j\), and the selection rule \(P_j\), subject to the polarized-ray (or remedial) restriction above. Let \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y: y\in F_j\}\) be the support function of \(F_j\).

The large-market OSP design problem can be written as the convex program
\[\min_{(x_j)_{j\in J}}\ \sum_{j\in J}\pi_j\cdot x_j\quad\text{s.t.}\quad x_j\in F_j\ \forall j,\ \sum_{j\in J}x_j=(n_1,\dots,n_I),\]

\[\sup_{\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^I}\ \sum_{i=1}^I\lambda_i n_i-\sum_{j\in J}S_j(\lambda-\pi_j).\]
Evaluating or optimizing over the mechanism for each agent \(j\) therefore reduces to characterizing the support function \(S_j\), i.e. solving an optimization problem whose decision variables are menus of polarized rays and selection rules.

Determine whether the optimization problem of characterizing \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y: y\in F_j\}\) (equivalently, optimizing over polarized-ray menus and selection rules for a given agent \(j\) in the large-market relaxation) can be solved efficiently as a function of \((w,\eta_j,\text{the distribution of }c_j)\). In particular, establish the computational complexity of computing \(S_j(w)\) (e.g. polynomial-time solvable vs. NP-hard) and/or design an algorithm with provable complexity guarantees for approximating \(S_j(w)\) to a specified accuracy.
open_problem_quote: Despite these simplifications, the extent to which \(S_j\) can be efficiently characterized remains an open question, which we leave for future work.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is primarily an algorithmic/complexity classification question (e.g., proving NP-hardness or designing a polynomial-time algorithm), not a conjecture about existence of a finite counterexample instance. Automated searches may help guess hardness via reductions but do not directly yield a definitive resolution.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate algorithms for approximating \(S_j(w)\) can be represented as code and evaluated on simulated instances with a computable objective value as a fitness signal. However, the ultimate goal concerns worst-case efficiency/complexity guarantees, which requires proof beyond empirical performance, so automated evolution would be supportive rather than decisive.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper introduces a large-market relaxation of optimal obviously strategy-proof (OSP) trading-mechanism design in a multi-task allocation problem with outside-option endowments \(\eta_j\). Using Theorem 2, it restricts attention to polarized-ray menus (or a remedial menu) and shows that the large-market design problem decomposes into an outer convex program with dual \(\sup_{\lambda}\ \sum_i \lambda_i n_i-\sum_j S_j(\lambda-\pi_j)\), where \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y:y\in F_j\}\) is the support function of the set of expected assignments achievable for agent \(j\) by varying polarized-ray menus and selection rules. Thus, computational tractability hinges on evaluating/optimizing \(S_j\) for given \(w\), \(\eta_j\), and the distribution of \(c_j\).

As of the source paper (2026), the computational complexity of characterizing \(S_j(w)\) is explicitly left open: while the polarized-ray characterization yields a strong dimension reduction (menu size at most \(I+1\)) and separability across agents in the dual, the remaining inner optimization over ray directions and selection rules is still a difficult nonconvex design problem. Related work on computational aspects of OSP (Golowich and Li) and on hardness/duality in multi-dimensional mechanism design (Daskalakis–Deckelbaum–Tzamos; Hart–Nisan) suggests that intractability is plausible in general, while posted-price/prophet-inequality techniques (Feldman–Gravin–Lucier; D{"u}tting et al.) point toward approximation algorithms under additional structure. However, no paper in the provided corpus resolves whether \(S_j(w)\) is polynomial-time computable or NP-hard in the general polarized-ray setting, nor provides a general-purpose approximation scheme with guarantees.

Promising directions include: (i) identifying special cases where \(S_j\) reduces to a tractable convex program (e.g. \(I=2\), or distributions with monotone hazard/regularity properties akin to the two-task case referenced in the source paper); (ii) proving hardness by reduction from knapsack/partition-like problems encoded in the choice of ray directions and cutoffs in type space; and (iii) developing approximation algorithms by discretizing the type hypercube and restricting to a finite candidate set of ray directions, with error bounds translating into additive/multiplicative approximation of \(S_j(w)\) and hence of the outer dual objective.

sources.title: Obviously Strategy-Proof Multi-Dimensional Allocation and the Value of Choice
sources.theorem_or_result: Characterizes OSP trading mechanisms as (effectively) polarized-ray menus (Theorem 2), derives a large-market relaxation whose dual reduces mechanism optimization to computing support functions \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y:y\in F_j\}\), and explicitly leaves the computational complexity of evaluating \(S_j\) as an open question.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open problem: in the large-market dual, optimizing OSP mechanisms requires characterizing \(S_j(w)\) for each agent, i.e. optimizing over polarized-ray menus and selection rules; Section 4.2 states that efficient characterization remains open.

sources.title: On the computational properties of obviously strategy-proof mechanisms
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies algorithmic/computational aspects of OSP implementation, identifying structural restrictions and computational barriers in various mechanism classes (context-dependent).
sources.connection: Provides general computational perspective on OSP mechanisms; potentially relevant for proving hardness or designing algorithms for optimizing within restricted OSP families such as polarized-ray menus, though it does not directly analyze the support-function problem \(S_j(w)\).

sources.title: The complexity of optimal mechanism design
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows computational intractability phenomena for optimal mechanism design in multi-dimensional settings (hardness results under various formulations).
sources.connection: Suggests that optimizing over multi-dimensional mechanisms can be computationally hard; may inform NP-hardness conjectures for computing \(S_j(w)\) when the menu/selection-rule optimization encodes hard combinatorial structure, though the model here is without transfers and with polarized-ray restrictions.

sources.title: Strong duality for a multiple-good monopolist
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops duality-based approaches for multi-good mechanism design, enabling convex-analytic formulations in certain settings.
sources.connection: Methodologically related: the source paper’s large-market relaxation uses convex duality and support functions \(S_j\); this work provides precedent for duality tools, though not directly for polarized-ray menu optimization.

sources.title: Combinatorial auctions via posted prices
sources.theorem_or_result: Designs posted-price mechanisms for combinatorial allocation with approximation guarantees under certain valuation models.
sources.connection: Polarized-ray menus resemble restricted posted-price/linear-trade offers; posted-price approximation techniques may inspire polynomial-time approximation schemes for \(S_j(w)\) under additional structure on the cost distribution or task types.

sources.title: Posted prices, smoothness, and combinatorial prophet inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: Connects posted-price mechanisms to prophet inequalities and smoothness, yielding approximation results for online/uncertain allocation problems.
sources.connection: May provide approximation frameworks for large-market allocation with uncertainty; could be adapted to approximate \(S_j(w)\) when polarized-ray menus can be interpreted as posted-price trades along directions.

sources.title: Selling multiple correlated goods: Revenue maximization and menu-size complexity
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies complexity arising from menu size in multi-dimensional mechanism design; shows large menus may be required for near-optimality in some settings.
sources.connection: Although the polarized-ray characterization bounds menu size by \(I+1\), the paper highlights that even restricted menus can face nontrivial computational/approximation issues; provides conceptual backdrop for complexity questions about computing \(S_j(w)\).
